documents.title: Artificial Intelligence
documents.content: Artificial Intelligence (AI) refers to the simulation of human intelligence processes by computer systems. These processes include learning, reasoning, problem-solving, perception, and language understanding. AI systems can be trained to perform specific tasks by processing large amounts of data and identifying patterns.

documents.title: Machine Learning
documents.content: Machine Learning is a subset of artificial intelligence that enables systems to learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed. ML algorithms build a mathematical model based on sample data, known as training data, to make predictions or decisions without following explicit instructions for the task.

documents.title: Deep Learning
documents.content: Deep Learning is a subset of machine learning based on artificial neural networks with multiple layers (hence 'deep'). The network learns to recognize patterns in data by adjusting the weights of connections between neurons. Deep learning is particularly effective for image recognition, natural language processing, and speech recognition tasks.

documents.title: Natural Language Processing
documents.content: Natural Language Processing (NLP) is a field of artificial intelligence that focuses on the interaction between computers and human language. NLP techniques enable computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language in a meaningful and useful way. Applications include machine translation, sentiment analysis, and chatbots.

documents.title: Vector Embedding
documents.content: Vector embedding is a representation of data (text, images, or other objects) as a list of numbers in a high-dimensional space. Words or documents are mapped to vectors such that semantically similar items are close to each other in the vector space. This representation enables efficient similarity search and machine learning operations.

documents.title: Semantic Search
documents.content: Semantic search refers to search algorithms that consider the meaning and intent behind query terms rather than exact keyword matches. Unlike traditional keyword search, semantic search understands context, synonyms, and relationships between words. It uses vector embeddings and similarity metrics to retrieve relevant results based on conceptual similarity.

documents.title: Transformer Model
documents.content: A Transformer is a deep learning architecture introduced in 2017 that uses self-attention mechanisms to process sequences of data in parallel rather than sequentially. Transformers are the foundation of modern language models like BERT and GPT. They excel at capturing long-range dependencies and contextual relationships in text.

documents.title: BERT
documents.content: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is a transformer-based language model pre-trained on large text corpora. It uses bidirectional training to understand context from both left and right sides of a word. BERT has become a foundation for many NLP tasks including question answering, sentiment analysis, and semantic similarity.

documents.title: Cross-Encoder
documents.content: A cross-encoder is a neural network architecture that takes pairs of inputs (query and document) and directly outputs a relevance score. Unlike bi-encoders that separately encode queries and documents, cross-encoders jointly process both inputs to model their interaction. This makes them more accurate for ranking tasks but computationally more expensive.

documents.title: Bi-Encoder
documents.content: A bi-encoder is a neural network architecture that separately encodes two inputs (query and document) into fixed-size vector representations. The similarity between them is computed using distance metrics like cosine similarity. Bi-encoders are computationally efficient and allow pre-computation of document embeddings for fast retrieval.

documents.title: ChromaDB
documents.content: Chroma is an open-source vector database designed for building AI applications with embeddings. It provides a simple API for storing, querying, and managing vector embeddings at scale. ChromaDB supports various indexing methods like HNSW for efficient similarity search and enables easy integration with machine learning workflows.

documents.title: Cosine Similarity
documents.content: Cosine similarity is a metric that measures the similarity between two non-zero vectors by calculating the cosine of the angle between them. It ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 means identical direction and 0 means orthogonal. In NLP and semantic search, cosine similarity is widely used to measure semantic closeness between word or document embeddings.

documents.title: API Key
documents.content: An API key is a unique identifier used to authenticate requests to an API. It serves as a credential that allows a client to access specific resources or services. API keys are commonly used for rate limiting, tracking usage, and controlling access to APIs. They should be kept secure and never exposed in public code.

documents.title: Multi-Tenancy
documents.content: Multi-tenancy is an architecture pattern where a single instance of an application serves multiple customers (tenants). Each tenant's data is isolated from others while sharing the same infrastructure and codebase. This approach reduces costs and simplifies maintenance while providing data security and customization capabilities for each tenant.

documents.title: RESTful API
documents.content: A RESTful API (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building web services that use HTTP requests to perform CRUD operations on resources. REST APIs are stateless, use standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE), and return data in formats like JSON. They are widely used for building scalable and interoperable web services.

documents.title: FastAPI
documents.content: FastAPI is a modern, fast web framework for building APIs with Python. It is based on standard Python type hints and uses Uvicorn as the ASGI server. FastAPI automatically generates interactive API documentation, provides built-in validation using Pydantic, and offers excellent performance suitable for production applications.

documents.title: Rate Limiting
documents.content: Rate limiting is a technique used to control the rate of requests a user or client can make to an API within a specified time window. It prevents abuse, ensures fair resource allocation, and protects servers from being overwhelmed. Common rate limiting strategies include token bucket, sliding window, and fixed window counters.

documents.title: Information Retrieval
documents.content: Information Retrieval (IR) is the field of study concerned with finding relevant information from large collections of unstructured data. IR systems use techniques like indexing, ranking, and relevance scoring to retrieve documents that match user queries. Modern IR systems leverage machine learning and semantic understanding for improved results.

documents.title: Tokenization
documents.content: Tokenization is the process of breaking down text into smaller units called tokens, which can be words, subwords, or characters. In NLP, tokenization is a crucial preprocessing step that converts raw text into a format suitable for machine learning models. Different tokenization strategies exist, including word-level, character-level, and subword tokenization (like BPE).

documents.title: Model Training
documents.content: Model training is the process of teaching a machine learning algorithm to recognize patterns in data by adjusting model parameters. During training, the algorithm iterates through labeled training data, makes predictions, compares them to actual values, and updates parameters to minimize error. Training quality depends on data quantity, quality, and appropriate hyperparameter tuning.
